sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An adrenaline autoinjector delivers a fixed IM dose of adrenaline for suspected anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction with evolving airway, breathing, or circulatory involvement. It is a rapid first-line method when an autoinjector is available or when bystander treatment has already been started.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm that anaphylaxis is suspected and expose the outer thigh if practical

2. Remove the device from its carrier and take off the safety cap according to device instructions

3. Press the injector firmly into the outer mid-thigh, through clothing if needed

4. Hold in place for the required activation time for that device, then remove it safely

5. Record the time and monitor for response in airway, breathing, perfusion, and conscious state

6. If symptoms persist or recur, repeat dosing may be required according to protocol while broader anaphylaxis management continues

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- The outer thigh is the preferred site because absorption is more reliable than the arm in anaphylaxis
- Do not allow the patient to stand or walk if they are hypotensive, faint, or clearly unwell
- An autoinjector does not replace oxygen, airway support, monitoring, and rapid transport
- Accidental injection into a finger or hand needs medical review because of vasoconstriction risk
